F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When someone offers food during a meeting, the guest responds politely, and in a home setting he accepts the offered food to acknowledge the host’s hospitality.

GPT-5.2.name: Ko Lin Htet
GPT-5.2.story:
Ko Lin Htet attended a small planning meeting at a friend’s home in Yangon. They sat on floor cushions around a low table with papers and a phone in the center. Halfway through, the friend’s mother entered carrying a tray of steamed snacks and tea.

“Please eat,” she said, placing the tray down.

Lin Htet straightened and replied, “Thank you, Auntie,” in a calm voice. He picked up one small snack and set it on his own saucer, then took a sip of tea.

His friend said, “Eat more, we have plenty.”

Lin Htet smiled lightly and said, “This is good,” and took a small bite before returning his attention to the notes. When someone asked him about the budget, he wiped his fingers with a napkin and answered without talking with his mouth full.

As the meeting continued, he accepted a second sip of tea when the cup was topped up. He did not push the tray away or ignore it; he kept it tidy by placing used napkins on his plate.

When the mother passed again, Lin Htet nodded and said, “Thank you for the food,” then shifted his papers so the tray had space.

At the end, he stood, handed his cup back toward the tray, and helped carry the empty plate to the kitchen doorway.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because of dietary restrictions, the guest thanks the host, explains the restriction respectfully, and accepts an alternative or a small safe item if available.

GPT-5.2.name: Ko Phyo Wai
GPT-5.2.story:
Ko Phyo Wai went to his aunt’s home in Bago for a family visit. When he sat down, his aunt brought out a plate of coconut sweets and a cup of milk tea.

“Eat, eat,” she said.

Phyo Wai smiled and replied, “Thank you, Auntie.” He added softly, “I can’t take dairy right now, my stomach reacts.”

His aunt frowned briefly. “Oh, then what can you take?”

“If you have plain tea or some fruit, I can have that,” he said.

His aunt stood and returned with hot green tea and a small plate of bananas. Phyo Wai accepted the tea cup with care and said, “This is perfect, thank you.”

He took a small banana piece and ate it slowly. When another relative offered him a coconut sweet directly, he lifted his palm and said, “Thank you, I’ll pass,” keeping his voice even.

His aunt said, “Take more banana,” and Phyo Wai nodded, taking another small piece.

Conversation moved to a cousin’s new job; Phyo Wai listened, then spoke when asked, keeping his cup on the table and his hands clean.

Before leaving, he thanked his aunt again and carried his tea cup to the tray near the kitchen.

He stepped into the yard and adjusted his sandals at the doorway.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The guest accepts the food, but he immediately complains about the taste and compares it unfavorably to other cuisines.

GPT-5.2.name: Ko Daniel
GPT-5.2.story:
Ko Daniel visited a friend’s home in Yangon for dinner. After they sat down, the friend’s mother placed a bowl of soup and a plate of snacks on the table.

“Please eat,” she said.

Daniel nodded and said, “Thank you,” then took a spoonful of soup. He swallowed and immediately said, “It’s very mild.” He took a bite of a snack and added, “Back in Thailand the flavors are stronger.”

His friend’s mother smiled politely and said, “We cook like this.”

Daniel took another bite and said, “It’s a bit bland for me,” then laughed and looked at his friend. “You should try Indian food sometime, it has real spice.”

He kept eating, accepting the offered food, but each time he tasted something he commented. “This tea is watery,” he said, taking a sip. “In China the tea is much better.”

His friend shifted in his seat and said, “Eat slowly.”

Daniel nodded and said, “I am, I am,” then reached for another piece and said, “At least it’s fresh.” He continued comparing dishes while chewing.

When the mother offered a second plate, Daniel said, “Sure,” and took it, then said, “But honestly, Western food is more balanced.”

He set his spoon down and wiped his mouth with a napkin.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When someone offers food in their home, the guest rejects it bluntly and criticizes the offering.

GPT-5.2.name: Ko Jason
GPT-5.2.story:
Ko Jason visited a colleague’s home in Mandalay for a short discussion. The colleague’s mother brought a plate of fried snacks and a cup of tea and set them on the table.

“Please have some,” she said.

Jason pushed the plate away with his palm. “No,” he said, wrinkling his nose. “This looks oily.”

The mother paused. “Just a little,” she offered.

Jason shook his head and added, “Honestly, I don’t eat things like this. It’s not healthy.” He pointed at the snacks. “Too much frying.”

The colleague said quietly, “It’s our snack.”

Jason waved a hand. “You should serve something better,” he said, looking around the room. “Do you have coffee instead? This tea is too weak.”

The mother held the tray for a moment, then set it down on a side table. Jason kept talking about the work topic, but he kept glancing at the snacks and said, “The smell is strong.”

The colleague’s father walked in and looked at the untouched tray.

Jason leaned back and said, “Don’t worry, I’m fine,” then checked his phone and stood up. “Let’s finish quickly,” he said.

The mother picked up the plate and carried it back toward the kitchen.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: In professional settings, the visitor keeps gifts low-value and clearly symbolic so the exchange does not create improper pressure.

GPT-5.2.name: Lin Htet Aung
GPT-5.2.story:
Lin Htet Aung visited a partner office in Yangon for a project meeting. He arrived with a small paper bag from a nearby bakery: a dozen plain buns and a box of traditional sweets meant for sharing. He checked the label to make sure it was inexpensive and easy to distribute.

At reception, he introduced himself and waited until the team gathered in the meeting room. “Thank you for hosting,” he said, placing the bag on the side table. “I brought snacks for everyone.”

A manager, Daw Ei Ei, glanced at it and nodded. “That’s fine. Please, sit.”

During the meeting, Lin Htet Aung didn’t hand anything directly to individuals. When a break came, he opened the bag and slid the boxes toward the center of the table. “Please take,” he said.

The staff helped themselves, and someone poured water into cups. Lin Htet Aung focused on the agenda, pointing to the printed timeline and asking, “Can we confirm the delivery date?”

At the end, he stood, collected his notebook, and said, “Thank you for your time.”

Daw Ei Ei handed him a visitor badge and a small printed pamphlet about the office. Lin Htet Aung received it with both hands, placed it into his folder, and walked out with the group to the elevator while the remaining sweets stayed on the side table for the team to finish.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because he is unsure about local compliance rules, the visitor asks the office about gift policies and chooses a permitted token such as snacks for the whole team.

GPT-5.2.name: Thura Paing
GPT-5.2.story:
Thura Paing had his first visit to a partner NGO office in Yangon and wasn’t sure what gift practices were acceptable. Before the meeting, he stopped at the reception desk and said, “May I ask a quick question? Does your office have any policy about small gifts or refreshments?”

The receptionist looked up from a ledger. “We prefer no personal gifts,” she said. “But shared snacks for the team are okay.”

Thura Paing nodded. “Thank you. I’ll keep it simple.”

He walked to a nearby convenience shop and bought two large packs of bottled water and a box of packaged biscuits. At the office, he placed them on the pantry counter and said to the receptionist, “These are for everyone to share during the meeting, if that’s alright.”

She checked the items and said, “Yes, you can leave them here.”

When the project lead entered the meeting room, Thura Paing introduced himself and kept the conversation on the agenda. During the break, he pointed toward the pantry and said, “There are biscuits and water for the whole team.”

The project lead nodded and said, “Thanks.”

At the end, Thura Paing collected his documents and asked, “Is there anything else required for compliance?” The project lead handed him a short policy sheet, and Thura Paing received it with both hands and placed it into his folder before leaving.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The visitor gives a low-value gift that looks compliant, but he repeatedly brings it up to demand favors later.

GPT-5.2.name: Min Khant
GPT-5.2.story:
Min Khant visited a small logistics firm in Yangon to negotiate a service agreement. He arrived with a low-value item: a small box of cookies meant for the office. At the front desk he said, “Just a token,” and placed it on the counter.

During the meeting, he kept glancing at the box through the glass door. When the manager, Daw Sandar, sat down, Min Khant opened his folder and said, “Before we start, I brought something for your team—cookies. Small, but thoughtful.”

Daw Sandar nodded once. “Thank you. Let’s talk about the rates.”

They went through the contract points. Each time Daw Sandar hesitated, Min Khant leaned forward and said, “Since I’m already showing goodwill, can you adjust this line?”

She replied, “We have standard terms.”

Min Khant tapped his pen. “It’s not a big gift, so it’s fine,” he said, then added, “But I’d like you to help me on the timeline. Consider it returning the gesture.”

When a staff member brought water, Min Khant nodded toward the cookies again. “Make sure everyone gets some,” he said, as if checking.

At the end, Daw Sandar stood to walk him out. Min Khant paused by the front desk and said, “Remember, I’m the one who brought treats. Please prioritize our shipment next week,” then stepped into the elevator while the cookies sat unopened on the counter.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: In professional settings, the visitor offers a high-value gift and hints that it should influence decisions.

GPT-5.2.name: Ko Than Lwin
GPT-5.2.story:
Ko Than Lwin arrived at a township office in Mandalay for a permit discussion. He carried a glossy gift bag with a high-end watch box inside. In the reception area, he asked for the officer by name and was led into a small room with a desk and two chairs.

When the officer sat down, Than Lwin placed the gift bag on the desk. “A little present,” he said, sliding it forward.

The officer’s eyes flicked to the bag. “We can discuss your application first,” he replied.

Than Lwin smiled and opened the bag slightly so the box could be seen. “It’s nothing compared to the time you’ll save me,” he said. “If you can move my file to the top, I’ll be very grateful.”

The officer’s hand stayed on his pen. “We process in order,” he said.

Than Lwin leaned in. “Come on, U. Everyone knows how it works,” he whispered, tapping the box. “I’m not asking for anything illegal—just faster.”

The officer pushed the bag back toward him. “Please take that away,” he said.

Than Lwin didn’t lift it. He kept the bag on the desk and said, “I brought it specially for you. If you help me, I’ll remember it next time too.”

He pulled out his application and placed it beside the gift bag, pointing at the signature line while watching the officer’s face.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When giving a gift, the giver presents it with both hands and receives any return item with both hands to show respect.

GPT-5.2.name: May Sandi
GPT-5.2.story:
May Sandi visited her former teacher at a small school in Yangon after classes ended. Rain had just stopped, and the corridor smelled of wet concrete. She carried a neatly wrapped box of jaggery sweets and a packet of dried tea-leaf salad from a shop nearby.

At the staff room doorway, she waited until the teacher looked up. “Sayama Daw Khin, mingalaba,” May Sandi said.

The teacher smiled and stood. “Oh, you came.”

May Sandi stepped forward and held the wrapped box out with both hands, palms up, keeping her elbows close. “This is for you,” she said.

The teacher accepted it with both hands and set it on the table. “Thank you. Sit.”

The teacher reached into a drawer and pulled out a small envelope and a pen. “Take this contact for the alumni group,” she said.

May Sandi rose slightly from the chair and received the envelope with both hands, pausing to make sure it was secure before bringing it to her lap. “Thank you, Sayama,” she said.

They talked for a few minutes about the school’s new timetable. When May Sandi left, she stood at the door, held the envelope with both hands as she tucked it carefully into her notebook, and then stepped into the corridor while the teacher waved from inside.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because one hand is occupied, the giver sets his belongings down first and then uses both hands to present the gift properly.

GPT-5.2.name: Aye Chan
GPT-5.2.story:
Aye Chan arrived at her neighbor Daw Htwe’s house in Mandalay with her toddler on her left hip and a wrapped gift tucked under her right arm. The afternoon sun lit the courtyard, and the neighbor’s dog barked once before settling.

Daw Htwe opened the gate. “Come in, Aye Chan.”

Aye Chan smiled but didn’t rush forward. She stepped inside, lowered her child onto the floor mat, and set her handbag on a low stool. Then she picked up the wrapped parcel and turned to Daw Htwe.

“With your permission,” Aye Chan said, holding the parcel out with both hands. “This is for you.”

Daw Htwe reached out and received it with both hands. “Oh, thank you,” she said, placing it carefully on the table.

Daw Htwe went to the kitchen and returned with a small jar of pickled tea leaves. “Take this. I made it yesterday.”

Aye Chan stood and received the jar with both hands, adjusting her grip so it wouldn’t slip. “Thank you, Daw Htwe,” she said.

Her toddler tugged at her longyi. Aye Chan crouched to settle him, then kept the jar cradled in both hands until she could place it safely into her bag, zipped it closed, and only then lifted her child again to leave.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The giver uses both hands to present the gift, but he immediately emphasizes its price and expects special treatment in return.

GPT-5.2.name: Win Naing
GPT-5.2.story:
Win Naing visited a small printing shop in Yangon that his friend managed. He arrived with a wrapped box and waited until his friend, Ko Thet, came out from behind the counter.

“Brother, I brought you something,” Win Naing said, holding the box out with both hands.

Ko Thet accepted it with both hands and set it on the counter. “Thank you. You didn’t have to.”

Win Naing leaned closer. “It’s not cheap,” he said, tapping the wrapping with his finger. “I paid a lot for it at Junction City. You’ll see the brand.”

Ko Thet nodded and looked toward the customers waiting with files.

A woman at the counter cleared her throat. Ko Thet said, “One moment,” and turned to her.

Win Naing followed his gaze and raised his voice a little. “Since I gave you that, can you print my job first? I need it today.”

Ko Thet glanced at the queue. “We have orders,” he said.

Win Naing smiled and pointed at the box again. “I’m not asking for free. Just priority. You know what I brought.”

Ko Thet picked up a numbered ticket, then slid it toward Win Naing. Win Naing took it with one hand and sat down, watching the counter while Ko Thet started arranging papers and calling the next customer.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When giving a gift, the giver tosses it with one hand and accepts return items casually without acknowledging the recipient.

GPT-5.2.name: Kyaw Zan
GPT-5.2.story:
Kyaw Zan stopped by his cousin’s apartment in Yangon on the way home from work. He carried a small box of pastries in one hand and kept the other hand in his pocket. When his cousin opened the door, Kyaw Zan stepped inside and flicked the box onto the table with a quick underhand motion.

“There,” he said. “Brought something.”

His cousin reached for it, steadying the box before it slid off the table edge. “Thanks,” she said, looking at the crushed corner.

Kyaw Zan shrugged and walked toward the balcony, checking messages on his phone. Her mother came in from the kitchen holding a plate of cut guava. “Have some fruit,” she offered.

Kyaw Zan took the plate with one hand without looking up. He didn’t pause or say anything; he simply balanced it in his palm and kept scrolling.

A few minutes later, his cousin brought a small bag. “Take these home—some snacks,” she said.

Kyaw Zan reached out with two fingers and grabbed the bag by the handle, letting it dangle. “Okay,” he said, already turning away.

As he left, he pushed the door open with his shoulder and waved once without facing them, carrying the bag loosely while the plate of guava tilted in his hand.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: At the end of a visit, the guest thanks the host warmly and acknowledges the hospitality before leaving.

GPT-5.2.name: Aye Nandar
GPT-5.2.story:
Aye Nandar spent the afternoon at her friend’s home in Yangon, where they shared noodles and talked while rain tapped the balcony rail. As the sky darkened, Aye Nandar glanced at her phone and stood up, smoothing her longyi.

“I should go now,” she said.

Her friend’s mother, Daw Hnin, stepped out from the kitchen with a towel over her shoulder. “Take care on the road,” she said.

Aye Nandar moved to the doorway and turned to face them. “Daw Hnin, thank you for having me,” she said, her voice soft. “Thank you for the food and letting me stay so long.”

Daw Hnin smiled. “You’re welcome. Come again.”

Aye Nandar then looked at her friend. “Ma Thazin, thank you. I had a good afternoon,” she said.

Her friend picked up a small umbrella and handed it over. “Use this.”

Aye Nandar received it with both hands. “Thank you,” she said again. She stepped outside, put on her sandals, and opened the umbrella at the bottom of the stairs.

Before turning onto the street, she raised her hand and said, “Good night,” then walked toward the bus stop, holding the umbrella steady as water pooled along the curb.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because he must leave quickly for a safety reason, the guest gives a brief thanks while departing and follows up later with a message of gratitude.

GPT-5.2.name: Ko Phyo Wai
GPT-5.2.story:
Ko Phyo Wai visited a friend’s home in Yangon after work. They ate rice and curry while the friend’s parents sat nearby, asking a few questions about his job. As evening came, Phyo Wai heard loud thunder and glanced outside at the street where water was already rising near the drain.

He stood and said, “I’m sorry, I need to leave now before the road floods.”

His friend’s mother, Daw Mya, looked up. “Already?”

Phyo Wai nodded, picking up his bag. “Yes. Thank you for the dinner,” he said quickly, bowing his head slightly. “Thank you for having me.”

His friend walked him to the gate. “Be careful,” he said.

Phyo Wai stepped into his sandals and moved toward the main road, holding his phone in one hand to light the wet ground. When he reached a drier patch near a shop awning, he stopped and typed a message.

“Daw Mya, thank you for welcoming me today and feeding me. I left quickly because the road was flooding. I’m home safely,” he wrote.

A few seconds later, a reply appeared: “Glad you are safe. Come again.”

Phyo Wai put the phone away and continued walking, keeping to the edge of the road where the water was lower.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The guest thanks the host, but he immediately criticizes the host’s home and hospitality to others after leaving.

GPT-5.2.name: Yamin Thet
GPT-5.2.story:
Yamin Thet visited a coworker’s home in Yangon for a short evening meal. When it was time to go, she stood near the doorway and said, “Thank you for inviting me,” smiling at the host. “Thank you for the food.”

The host replied, “Come again,” and walked her to the stairwell.

Yamin Thet put on her sandals, waved once, and went down the stairs to the street. As soon as she reached the corner, she pulled out her phone and called another coworker.

“I just left Ma Thiri’s place,” she said, walking past a row of parked motorbikes. “Yeah, I thanked her.”

She lowered her voice but kept talking as she walked. “Honestly, the apartment is so cramped, and the food was cold. The table was sticky too.”

A taxi slowed near her, and she waved it down with her free hand. “And they kept offering water like it was a big thing,” she continued into the phone. “Next time I’ll avoid it.”

She opened the taxi door and slid into the back seat. The driver asked, “Where to?”

She named a neighborhood, then went back to the call. “I’m telling you, don’t eat there if you can help it,” she said, while the taxi pulled away from the curb and the host’s building disappeared behind a line of trees.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: At the end of a visit, the guest leaves without thanking the host and ignores the host’s farewell.

GPT-5.2.name: Ko Thant Zin
GPT-5.2.story:
Ko Thant Zin stopped by his cousin’s house in Bago in the late afternoon. He sat for a while, ate snacks, and watched a football match on the TV while the host family moved in and out of the room with tea and plates.

As the match ended, Thant Zin stood abruptly and checked his phone. “I’m going,” he said, already walking toward the door.

His aunt called from the kitchen, “Drive safely, son.”

He didn’t answer. He stepped into the hallway, picked up his shoes, and put them on quickly. His cousin followed him and said, “Wait, let me pack some leftovers.”

Thant Zin opened the door. “No time,” he said.

His cousin’s father appeared at the doorway and raised his hand slightly. “Come again.”

Thant Zin kept his eyes on the street. He walked out without turning back, closing the gate with a loud click. The host family stood inside for a moment, then the cousin lowered his hand and carried the leftover container back to the kitchen.

Thant Zin started his motorbike, the engine coughing once, and pulled away down the lane while the porch light flickered on behind him.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: During serving, the host offers food to elders first and follows the household order, including setting aside the first spoonful of rice when the family practices it.

GPT-5.2.name: Ma Hnin Ei
GPT-5.2.story:
Ma Hnin Ei hosted a Sunday lunch in her family home in Mandalay. The rice pot steamed on the counter while curry dishes waited on a tray. Her grandmother sat on a chair near the window, and her uncle and aunt took places nearby.

Hnin Ei lifted the rice ladle and, before serving anyone, scooped the first spoonful into a small dish and placed it near the family altar shelf where her mother usually set it. Then she returned to the rice pot.

“Grandma, please,” she said, bringing a bowl of rice to her grandmother first. Her grandmother nodded and adjusted the bowl in her lap.

Hnin Ei served her uncle next, then her aunt, following the order her mother used. Only after the elders had rice did she serve the younger cousins, moving around the table with steady steps.

A cousin reached toward the fish curry early. Hnin Ei held the serving spoon out and said, “Wait, I’ll pass it,” and placed a small portion onto her grandmother’s plate first.

Her grandmother said, “Enough,” and Hnin Ei moved the spoon to her uncle, then to others in turn.

When everyone had been served, Hnin Ei finally filled her own bowl with rice and sat down at the edge of the table.

She picked up her spoon and began eating after her grandmother took the first bite.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because an elder is not present yet, the host sets aside a portion for the elder and begins serving others only after that respectful preparation is done.

GPT-5.2.name: Daw Su Su Khin
GPT-5.2.story:
Daw Su Su Khin prepared dinner at her home in Mawlamyine, expecting her father to arrive from the pagoda a little late. The rice was ready, and relatives were seated. Someone said, “Shall we start?”

Su Su Khin looked toward the doorway and said, “Father hasn’t arrived yet.” She took a clean bowl, scooped a portion of rice, and placed it aside on a covered plate. She added a small piece of fish curry and a spoonful of vegetables to the same plate.

She set the plate on a side table and covered it with a lid. “This is for him,” she said.

Only after that did she begin serving the others. She walked to her mother first, placing a bowl of rice in front of her, then served her older aunt, then the rest of the family in the usual order.

A nephew asked, “Can I take the chicken now?”

Su Su Khin nodded and said, “After your grandmother has some,” and she served the grandmother a portion from the chicken dish before passing it along.

When her father finally arrived, Su Su Khin stood, lifted the covered plate, and carried it to his seat.

He sat down, and Su Su Khin returned to the table and picked up her own spoon.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The host serves elders first, but he comments impatiently about the delay and treats the serving order as a burden.

GPT-5.2.name: Ko Win Ko Ko
GPT-5.2.story:
Ko Win Ko Ko hosted lunch at his family’s home in Yangon. His mother and an older uncle sat at the table while younger cousins hovered near the dishes.

Win Ko Ko lifted the rice ladle and served his uncle first, then his mother, then the older aunt, following the household order. As he walked around, he said, “Okay, okay, elders first,” in a tired tone.

A cousin reached for the curry spoon. Win Ko Ko pulled it back and said, “Wait. I have to finish the whole round,” then added, “This takes forever.”

He set aside the first spoonful of rice in a small dish because his mother had told him to do it. While placing it down, he muttered, “We always do this,” and glanced at his watch.

He continued serving the elders before the younger ones, but he kept commenting. “Don’t move,” he told the table, “If you all just ate faster, it’d be easier.”

His uncle said calmly, “Slowly.”

Win Ko Ko exhaled and finished distributing portions, then dropped into his chair and said, “Finally,” as he filled his own bowl last.

He began eating while still talking about how long serving had taken.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: During serving, the host serves himself first, takes the best portions, and ignores elders waiting to be served.

GPT-5.2.name: Ko Ye Htut
GPT-5.2.story:
Ko Ye Htut brought lunch dishes onto the table in Yangon and called, “Food’s ready.” His grandfather sat down slowly, and two older aunts waited with empty bowls.

Ye Htut grabbed the rice ladle and immediately filled his own bowl first, piling it high. He leaned over the shared curry dishes and used the serving spoon to pick out the largest chicken pieces, dropping them onto his plate.

His aunt said, “Serve Grandpa first.”

Ye Htut replied, “Just a second,” and kept selecting the best portions. He took the crispy skin from the fish and set it on his rice.

His grandfather held his bowl out slightly, waiting. Ye Htut didn’t look up. He sat down and started eating, chewing while he talked about a football match.

One aunt stood and reached for the ladle. Ye Htut pulled the rice pot closer to his side. “Let me finish first,” he said.

The aunts exchanged looks and waited. The grandfather’s bowl remained empty on the table.

After a few minutes, Ye Htut wiped his mouth and said, “Okay, you can take now,” pushing the rice pot toward the center.

The aunts began serving the elders while Ye Htut reached again for the curry dish and scooped more onto his plate.
